title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a designer called Lucy talking about a sustainable fashion project. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a designer called Lucy talking about a sustainable fashion project. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: A designer called Lucy introduces a sustainable fashion project.
extracts.audio_script: Hello everyone. I’m Lucy and I lead Green Threads, a project where young people learn sewing and design while finding new uses for unwanted clothing. We began in the back room of a clothes shop, but our classes attracted more people than expected. We now work from an empty [9]railway office[/9] beside the station, which the council offered us cheaply after it had been unused for years. Its large tables and bright windows make it a surprisingly good workshop, and people can reach it easily by public transport.||Green Threads is not a course for students who already make clothes. Beginners are welcome and will be shown every technique carefully. Some people assume our most common donated items are dresses or coats. In fact, local families give us far more pairs of [10]jeans[/10] than anything else, partly because the material is strong and easy to reuse in bags and other products. At the first session, students learn to operate sewing machines only after a basic lesson in [11]fabric cutting[/11], as measuring carefully avoids wasting material. Safety advice is included throughout.||Nothing expensive has to be purchased before joining. The project lends machines, needles and measuring equipment, and we have boxes of thread. Students should bring a [12]notebook[/12], because they will sketch ideas and record useful measurements during each meeting. In the opening weeks, everyone makes something simple. Instead of beginning with clothing that must fit exactly, new learners make a [13]shopping bag[/13] from reused fabric. It is practical, allows plenty of creative choices, and can usually be finished in two sessions. People often use it proudly afterwards.||We teach students to think carefully about where materials come from. In addition to donated clothing, we collect damaged fabric from a local [14]curtain factory[/14]; pieces too small for full products are often perfect for pockets or decorations. Completed items are displayed online, but our end-of-term event is physical rather than digital. Students will show their favourite designs in a small [15]fashion show[/15], held in the station hall for family and friends. Models will be volunteers from the group, not professionals, so the occasion should be relaxed and enjoyable.||Our specialist workshop last year covered repairing zips. This year, most learners voted for a session on [16]natural dyes[/16], using colour made from plants and food waste. Students who attend regularly and produce three finished pieces can build a [17]design portfolio[/17], with photographs and notes which may support applications for further study. Finally, we sell a limited number of products made during extra workshops. Money used to purchase new machines last year; this year it will support a [18]clothes exchange[/18] for local teenagers, where good-quality items can be swapped without cost. Applications for Green Threads close on Friday, and there are still a few places remaining.

dialogue.speaker: lucy
dialogue.text: Hello everyone. I’m Lucy and I lead Green Threads, a project where young people learn sewing and design while finding new uses for unwanted clothing. We began in the back room of a clothes shop, but our classes attracted more people than expected. We now work from an empty railway office beside the station, which the council offered us cheaply after it had been unused for years. Its large tables and bright windows make it a surprisingly good workshop, and people can reach it easily by public transport.||Green Threads is not a course for students who already make clothes. Beginners are welcome and will be shown every technique carefully. Some people assume our most common donated items are dresses or coats. In fact, local families give us far more pairs of jeans than anything else, partly because the material is strong and easy to reuse in bags and other products. At the first session, students learn to operate sewing machines only after a basic lesson in fabric cutting, as measuring carefully avoids wasting material. Safety advice is included throughout.||Nothing expensive has to be purchased before joining. The project lends machines, needles and measuring equipment, and we have boxes of thread. Students should bring a notebook, because they will sketch ideas and record useful measurements during each meeting. In the opening weeks, everyone makes something simple. Instead of beginning with clothing that must fit exactly, new learners make a shopping bag from reused fabric. It is practical, allows plenty of creative choices, and can usually be finished in two sessions. People often use it proudly afterwards.||We teach students to think carefully about where materials come from. In addition to donated clothing, we collect damaged fabric from a local curtain factory; pieces too small for full products are often perfect for pockets or decorations. Completed items are displayed online, but our end-of-term event is physical rather than digital. Students will show their favourite designs in a small fashion show, held in the station hall for family and friends. Models will be volunteers from the group, not professionals, so the occasion should be relaxed and enjoyable.||Our specialist workshop last year covered repairing zips. This year, most learners voted for a session on natural dyes, using colour made from plants and food waste. Students who attend regularly and produce three finished pieces can build a design portfolio, with photographs and notes which may support applications for further study. Finally, we sell a limited number of products made during extra workshops. Money used to purchase new machines last year; this year it will support a clothes exchange for local teenagers, where good-quality items can be swapped without cost. Applications for Green Threads close on Friday, and there are still a few places remaining.

This year's chosen specialist session is about (16) .
